title: Poulet rôti aux 40 gousses d'ail
subtitle: Provençal roast chicken with 40 cloves of unpeeled garlic
excerpt: A whole chicken roasted in a covered pot with 40 unpeeled cloves of garlic, white wine, and herbs. The garlic cooks inside its skins until completely soft, mild, and spreadable: nothing like raw garlic. Press it out of the skins onto bread to eat alongside the chicken. A Provençal classic that is simpler than it sounds.
sourceNotes:
Prosper Montagné, Larousse Gastronomique (1938), public domain edition. Poulet aux 40 gousses d'ail as a classic of Provençal cookery, the enclosed-pot method, and notes on the transformation of unpeeled garlic during long cooking.

La cuisinière provençale, J.-B. Reboul (1897), public domain. Garlic chicken and the principle of sealing the pot with a flour paste or tight lid to trap moisture and aroma.

glossaryTerms.definition: Garlic cooked slowly in fat or in a closed vessel until the cloves are completely soft and sweet, with none of the sharpness of raw garlic. The word confit means 'preserved' in French, but in modern usage it describes any ingredient slow-cooked until tender and caramelised.

content.text: This is the dish that surprises everyone who has not made it before: 40 cloves of garlic sounds like an assault, but the result is extraordinarily mild. Cooked unpeeled in a sealed pot for 90 minutes, the cloves turn to soft, sweet,

with none of the heat or bite of the raw bulb. You press them out of the skins at the table onto thick-cut bread. It is a Provençal farmhouse dish: one pot, no complicated technique, and a kitchen that smells extraordinary.

The enclosed cooking method is the reason the dish works. The casserole traps moisture and the garlic vapour circulates around the chicken throughout the cook, infusing the meat without ever making it sharp. Do not be tempted to open the lid during cooking: the sealed environment is what converts the garlic.

The casserole must be large enough for the chicken to sit inside with a lid that closes properly. A 26 to 28 cm cast-iron pot works well. You will need 3 to 4 whole heads of garlic to yield 40 cloves; do not peel them. Serve with thick-cut toasted bread for spreading the cooked garlic.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Put the {{garlic}} of unpeeled garlic cloves, {{thyme}} of thyme, {{rosemary}} of rosemary, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay into the base of the casserole. Pour over {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine. Season the inside of the chicken with salt and pepper. Set the chicken on top of the garlic, breast-side up, and season the outside generously.

Put the lid on tightly. Transfer to the oven. Roast for 1 hour 20 minutes without opening the lid. After 1 hour 20 minutes, remove the lid and return the pot to the oven for a further 15 minutes to colour the chicken skin. The chicken is done when the juices run clear when you pierce the thigh at its thickest point.

Remove the chicken to a board and rest for 10 minutes. Taste the pan juices and adjust the seasoning. Serve the chicken carved into portions with all the garlic cloves alongside. To eat the garlic, press the base of each clove between your fingers so the soft, pale interior slides out directly onto bread.

items.symptom: Chicken skin is pale and soft
items.cause: Lid left on for the whole cooking time
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 15 minutes as directed. If the skin is still pale, increase the oven to 220°C for the last 10 minutes.

items.symptom: Garlic is still firm after cooking
items.cause: Cloves cooked at too low a temperature or the seal was not tight
items.fix: The garlic needs the trapped steam to soften fully. Check the lid fits tightly; if not, seal the rim with a strip of foil before putting on the lid.

items.symptom: Pan juices are very pale and thin
items.cause: Too much wine or lid fitted too loosely allowing steam to escape
items.fix: Pour the juices into a small saucepan and reduce on the hob for 5 minutes. The garlic softening into the juices thickens them naturally; some reduction is always needed.

items.symptom: Chicken is not cooked through at the 1 hour 35 minute mark
items.cause: Larger bird or oven runs cool
items.fix: Return to the oven, lid off, for a further 15 minutes. Always check at the thigh joint: if the juices run pink, continue cooking.

content.text: The dish is Provençal in origin, from the same tradition as daube and other long, covered braises where aromatics and fat work together in an enclosed space. Garlic cooked in this way bears almost no resemblance to the raw bulb: the allicin that gives garlic its bite breaks down under heat, leaving only sweetness and depth. The recipe requires no particular skill beyond counting to forty. It has been a farmhouse Sunday dish across Provence for generations and shows up on bistro menus across France as a comforting, low-effort main course. A glass of Côtes de Provence rosé or a light Languedoc red works well alongside it.
